html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Seventeen — Part Two

The Book of Yesha'yahu

YESHA'YAHU

The Book of Isaiah — 1QIsa-a Great Isaiah Scroll

Chapters 13 through 27

The Oracles Against the Nations — Babylon Falls — The Great Apocalypse — Death Swallowed Up

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

1QIsa-a — The Great Isaiah Scroll — Cave 1 Qumran

The oldest and most complete prophetic manuscript in existence

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTERS 13 THROUGH 27 — ORACLES AGAINST NATIONS AND THE GREAT APOCALYPSE

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 13 The Oracle Concerning Babylon — The Day of YHWH Is Near — Babylon Shall Be Like Sodom

1 The oracle concerning Babylon that Yesha'yahu the son of Amoz saw.

2 On a bare mountain raise a signal — cry aloud to them, wave the hand for them to enter the gates of the nobles.

3 I myself have commanded my consecrated ones, and have summoned my mighty men to execute my anger, my proudly exulting ones.

4 The sound of a tumult is on the mountains as of a great multitude. The sound of an uproar of kingdoms, of nations gathering together. YHWH of hosts is mustering a host for battle.

5 They come from a distant land, from the end of the heavens, YHWH and the weapons of his indignation, to destroy the whole land.

6 Wail, for the day of YHWH is near — as destruction from the Almighty it will come.

7 Therefore all hands will be feeble, and every human heart will melt.

8 They will be dismayed — pangs and agony will seize them — they will be in anguish like a woman in labor. They will look aghast at one another — their faces will be aflame.

9 Behold, the day of YHWH comes, cruel, with wrath and fierce anger, to make the land a desolation and to destroy its sinners from it.

10 For the stars of the heavens and their constellations will not give their light. The sun will be dark at its rising, and the moon will not shed its light.

11 I will punish the world for its evil, and the wicked for their iniquity. I will put an end to the pomp of the arrogant, and lay low the pompous pride of the ruthless.

12 I will make people more rare than fine gold, and mankind than the gold of Ofir.

13 Therefore I will make the heavens tremble, and the earth will be shaken out of its place, at the wrath of YHWH of hosts in the day of his fierce anger.

14 And like a hunted gazelle, or like sheep with none to gather them, each will turn to his own people, and each will flee to his own land.

15 Whoever is found will be thrust through, and whoever is caught will fall by the sword.

16 Their infants will be dashed in pieces before their eyes. Their houses will be plundered and their wives ravished.

17 Behold, I am stirring up the Medes against them, who have no regard for silver and do not delight in gold.

18 Their bows will slaughter the young men. They will have no mercy on the fruit of the womb. Their eyes will not pity children.

19 And Babylon, the glory of kingdoms, the splendor and pomp of the Chaldeans, will be like Sodom and Gomorrah when Creator overthrew them.

20 It will never be inhabited or lived in for all generations. No Arab will pitch his tent there, no shepherds will make their flocks lie down there.

21 But wild animals will lie down there, and their houses will be full of howling creatures. There ostriches will dwell, and there wild goats will dance.

22 Hyenas will cry in its towers, and jackals in the pleasant palaces. Its time is close at hand and its days will not be prolonged.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 13 in full. The oracles against the nations — Chapters 13-23 — form a distinct collection within Yesha'yahu. The Babylon oracle opens this section with the most comprehensive Day of YHWH description in the prophetic corpus: cosmic darkening, universal military mobilization, and the reduction of human population rarer than Ofir gold.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 13 in full. Verse 19 — Babylon, the glory of kingdoms, the splendor and pomp of the Chaldeans, will be like Sodom and Gomorrah when Creator overthrew them — is read as the covenant declaration that no human civilization, however glorious, is exempt from the covenant standard. Babylon's glory does not protect it from Sodom's fate.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 13. Verse 17 — I am stirring up the Medes against them — is the covenant historical precision: the Babylonian empire was indeed overthrown by the Medes and Persians. The prophecy of Babylon's fall to the Medes is the covenant historical anchor of the entire oracle.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11 — I will punish the world for its evil and the wicked for their iniquity — I will put an end to the pomp of the arrogant and lay low the pompous pride of the ruthless. The Babylon oracle universalizes the covenant principle: YHWH's judgment against Babylon is simultaneously a declaration of his universal sovereignty over all nations. Babylon is not simply the enemy of Yisra'EL — it is the exemplar of human imperial arrogance against YHWH. Its fall is the covenant demonstration that no human power, however great, is exempt from divine accountability.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 14 The Fall of the King of Babylon — How You Have Fallen From Heaven — YHWH Will Have Compassion on Yaakov

1 For YHWH will have compassion on Yaakov and will again choose Yisra'EL, and will set them in their own land. And sojourners will join them and will attach themselves to the house of Yaakov.

2 And the peoples will take them and bring them to their place, and the house of Yisra'EL will possess them in YHWH's land as male and female slaves. They will take captive those who were their captors, and rule over those who oppressed them.

3 When YHWH has given you rest from your pain and turmoil and the hard service with which you were made to serve,

4 You will take up this taunt against the king of Babylon: How the oppressor has ceased, the insolent fury ceased.

5 YHWH has broken the staff of the wicked, the scepter of rulers,

6 That struck the peoples in wrath with unceasing blows, that ruled the nations in anger with unrelenting persecution.

7 The whole earth is at rest and quiet — they break forth into singing.

8 The cypresses rejoice at you, the cedars of Lebanon, saying, Since you were laid low, no woodcutter comes up against us.

9 Sheol beneath is stirred up to meet you when you come — it rouses the shades to greet you, all who were leaders of the earth. It raises from their thrones all who were kings of the nations.

10 All of them will respond and say to you: You too have become as weak as we. You have become like us.

11 Your pomp is brought down to Sheol, the sound of your harps. Maggots are laid as a bed beneath you, and worms are your covers.

12 How you have fallen from heaven, O Day Star, son of Dawn. How you are cut down to the ground, you who laid the nations low.

13 You said in your heart, I will ascend to heaven — above the stars of Creator I will set my throne on high. I will sit on the mount of assembly in the far reaches of the north.

14 I will ascend above the heights of the clouds — I will make myself like the Most High.

15 But you are brought down to Sheol, to the far reaches of the pit.

16 Those who see you will stare at you and ponder over you: Is this the man who made the earth tremble, who shook kingdoms,

17 Who made the world like a desert and overthrew its cities, who did not let his prisoners go home?

18 All the kings of the nations lie in glory, each in his own tomb.

19 But you are cast out, away from your grave, like a loathed branch, clothed with the slain, those pierced by the sword, who go down to the stones of the pit, like a trampled corpse.

20 You will not be joined with them in burial, because you have destroyed your land, you have slain your people. May the offspring of evildoers nevermore be named.

21 Prepare slaughter for his sons because of the guilt of their fathers, lest they rise and possess the earth, and fill the face of the world with cities.

22 I will rise up against them, declares YHWH of hosts, and will cut off from Babylon name and remnant, descendants and posterity, declares YHWH.

23 And I will make it a possession of the hedgehog, and pools of water, and I will sweep it with the broom of destruction, declares YHWH of hosts.

24 YHWH of hosts has sworn: As I have planned, so shall it be, and as I have purposed, so shall it stand —

25 That I will break the Assyrian in my land, and on my mountains trample him underfoot. And his yoke shall depart from them, and his burden from their shoulder.

26 This is the purpose that is purposed concerning the whole earth, and this is the hand that is stretched out over all the nations.

27 For YHWH of hosts has purposed, and who will annul it? His hand is stretched out, and who will turn it back?

28 In the year that King Akhaz died came this oracle:

29 Rejoice not, O Philistia, all of you, that the rod that struck you is broken, for from the serpent's root will come forth an adder, and its fruit will be a flying fiery serpent.

30 And the firstborn of the poor will graze, and the needy lie down in safety — but I will kill your root with famine, and your remnant it will slay.

31 Wail, O gate — cry out, O city — melt in fear, O Philistia, all of you. For smoke comes out of the north, and there is no straggler in his ranks.

32 What will one answer the messengers of the nation? YHWH has founded Tziyon, and in her the afflicted of his people find refuge.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 14 in full. Verses 12-15 — the taunt against the Day Star, son of Dawn — Helel ben Shakhar — is the covenant poetic address to the Babylonian king as the archetype of cosmic pride. 1QIsa-a preserves Helel clearly. The covenant irony: the one who aspired to ascend above the stars of Creator is brought to the pit.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 14 in full. Verse 1-3 — YHWH will have compassion on Yaakov and will again choose Yisra'EL — is read as the covenant anchor of the entire Babylon oracle: the judgment of Babylon is simultaneously the restoration of the covenant people. The fall of the oppressor and the return of the oppressed are covenant co-events.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 14. Verse 24-27 — YHWH of hosts has sworn — as I have planned, so shall it be — his hand is stretched out and who will turn it back? — is the covenant declaration of divine sovereignty that brackets the entire oracles against nations section. What YHWH purposes cannot be annulled.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12-15 — the taunt against the Day Star — how you have fallen from heaven — I will ascend to heaven — I will make myself like the Most High — but you are brought down to Sheol. The covenant principle of the entire oracles against nations: the one who aspires to divine status is brought to the deepest anti-divine location. The five I will declarations of the Babylonian king — verse 13-14 — are the covenant anatomy of the pride that provokes YHWH's judgment. Verse 32 — YHWH has founded Tziyon, and in her the afflicted of his people find refuge. The covenant response to every oracle against the nations: while empires fall, Tziyon stands as the refuge of the covenant community.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 15 The Oracle Concerning Moav — Ar of Moav Is Laid Waste — Weeping Throughout the Land

1 An oracle concerning Moav. Because Ar of Moav is laid waste in a night, Moav is undone. Because Kir of Moav is laid waste in a night, Moav is undone.

2 He has gone up to the temple, and to Divon, to the high places to weep. Over Nevo and over Medeiva Moav wails. On every head is baldness — every beard is shorn.

3 In the streets they wear sackcloth — on the housetops and in the squares everyone wails and melts in tears.

4 Kheshbon and El'aleh cry out, their voice is heard as far as Yahatz. Therefore the armed men of Moav cry aloud — his soul trembles.

5 My heart cries out for Moav — her fugitives flee to Tzoar, to Eglat-Shelishiyah. For at the ascent of Luhith they go up weeping. On the road to Khoronaim they raise a cry of destruction.

6 For the waters of Nimrim are a desolation — the grass is withered, the vegetation fails, the greenery is no more.

7 Therefore the abundance they have gained and what they have laid up they carry away over the Brook of the Willows.

8 For a cry has gone around the land of Moav — her wailing reaches to Eglaim, her wailing reaches to Beer-Elim.

9 For the waters of Dimon are full of blood — for I will bring upon Dimon even more, a lion for those of Moav who escape, and for the remnant of the land.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 15 in full. The Moav oracle is notable for the prophet's own grief — verse 5, my heart cries out for Moav. The covenant prophet weeps over the enemy nation's destruction. This compassion for covenant enemies within the oracles against nations is a distinctive feature of Yesha'yahu's prophetic theology.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 15 in full. The geographic precision of the Moav oracle — naming specific cities and routes — is read as the covenant specificity of prophetic vision: Yesha'yahu knows the land of Moav's topography in detail, suggesting direct prophetic knowledge beyond political intelligence.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 15. The oracle continues into Chapter 16, functioning as a single extended oracle of judgment and mercy toward Moav.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5 — my heart cries out for Moav. The prophet's grief for Moav's destruction is a covenant theological statement: YHWH's judgment against the nations does not produce covenant rejoicing in the prophet. The covenant prophet who declares judgment also weeps over its necessity. This is the same covenant emotional posture as Elisha weeping before Khaza'el in Melakhim Bet 8:11-12. The covenant oracle is not triumphalism.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 16 Send Lambs to the Ruler of the Land — Moav's Pride — In Three Years Moav Will Be Despised

1 Send the lamb to the ruler of the land, from Sela, by way of the desert, to the mount of the daughter of Tziyon.

2 Like fleeing birds, like a scattered nest, so are the daughters of Moav at the fords of the Arnon.

3 Give counsel, grant justice — make your shade like night at the height of noon. Shelter the outcasts, do not reveal the fugitive.

4 Let the outcasts of Moav sojourn among you — be a shelter to them from the destroyer. When the oppressor is no more, and destruction has ceased, and he who tramples underfoot has vanished from the land,

5 Then a throne will be established in steadfast love, and on it will sit in faithfulness in the tent of Dawid one who judges and seeks justice and is swift to do righteousness.

6 We have heard of the pride of Moav — how proud he is — of his arrogance, his pride, and his insolence. In his idle boasting he is not right.

7 Therefore let Moav wail for Moav, let everyone wail. Mourn, utterly stricken, for the raisin cakes of Kir-Khareseth.

8 For the fields of Kheshbon languish, and the vine of Sivmah — the lords of the nations have struck down its branches, which reached to Ya'zer and strayed to the desert. Its shoots spread abroad and passed over the sea.

9 Therefore I weep with the weeping of Ya'zer for the vine of Sivmah. I drench you with my tears, O Kheshbon and Elealeh, for over your summer fruit and your harvest the shout has ceased.

10 And joy and gladness are taken away from the fruitful field, and in the vineyards no songs are sung, no cheers are raised. No treader treads out wine in the presses — I have put an end to the shouting.

11 Therefore my inner parts moan like a lyre for Moav, and my inmost self for Kir-Khareseth.

12 And when Moav presents himself, when he wearies himself on the high place, when he comes to his sanctuary to pray, he will not prevail.

13 This is the word that YHWH spoke concerning Moav in the past.

14 But now YHWH has spoken, saying, In three years, like the years of a hired worker, the glory of Moav will be brought into contempt, in spite of all his great multitude, and those who remain will be very few and feeble.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 16 in full. Verse 5 — a throne will be established in steadfast love, and on it will sit in faithfulness in the tent of Dawid — is the covenant anchor embedded within the Moav oracle: even in the midst of judgment against a foreign nation, the covenant promise of the Davidic throne is reaffirmed.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 16 in full. Verses 9-11 — the prophet weeps for Moav's vineyards and his inner parts moan like a lyre — is read as the covenant revelation that prophetic judgment is not punitive satisfaction but prophetic grief. YHWH's covenant prophet weeps over the destruction of YHWH's enemies.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 16 largely consistent with the Masoretic. Verse 14 — in three years, like the years of a hired worker — the covenant precision of prophetic chronology. The detailed grief of verses 6-12 is not contradicted by the covenant judgment of verse 14. Both the grief and the judgment are true simultaneously.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3-5 — the oracle contains a covenant appeal: shelter the outcasts, do not reveal the fugitive, be a shelter to them — for then a throne will be established in steadfast love in the tent of Dawid. Moav is offered the covenant option of providing sanctuary for Yehudah's outcasts, and the covenant reward is the establishment of the just Davidic throne. The oracle against Moav contains within it a covenant invitation. Moav's pride — verse 6 — is what prevents the covenant response.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 17 The Oracle Concerning Damascus and Efrayim — Their Glory Will Become Like Thin Grain

1 An oracle concerning Damascus. Behold, Damascus will cease to be a city and will become a heap of ruins.

2 The cities of Aro'er are deserted — they will be for flocks, which will lie down, and none will make them afraid.

3 The fortress will disappear from Efrayim, and the kingdom from Damascus, and the remnant of Aram will be like the glory of the children of Yisra'EL, declares YHWH of hosts.

4 And in that day the glory of Yaakov will be brought low, and the fat of his flesh will grow lean.

5 And it shall be as when the reaper gathers standing grain and his arm harvests the ears, and as when one gleans the ears of grain in the Valley of Refa'im.

6 Gleanings will be left in it, as when an olive tree is beaten — two or three berries in the top of the highest bough, four or five on the branches of a fruit tree, declares YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL.

7 In that day man will look to his Maker, and his eyes will look on the Holy One of Yisra'EL.

8 He will not look to the altars, the work of his hands, and he will not look on what his own fingers have made, either the Asherim or the altars of incense.

9 In that day their strong cities will be like the deserted places of the wooded heights and the hilltops, which they deserted because of the children of Yisra'EL, and there will be desolation.

10 For you have forgotten the Creator of your salvation and have not remembered the Rock of your refuge. Therefore, though you plant pleasant plants and sow the vine branch of a stranger,

11 Though you make them grow on the day that you plant them, and make them blossom in the morning that you sow — yet the harvest will flee away in a day of grief and incurable pain.

12 Ah, the thunder of many peoples — they thunder like the thundering of the sea. Ah, the roar of nations — they roar like the roaring of mighty waters.

13 The nations roar like the roaring of many waters, but he will rebuke them, and they will flee far away, chased like chaff on the mountains before the wind and whirling dust before the storm.

14 At evening time, behold, terror. Before morning, they are no more. This is the portion of those who loot us, and the lot of those who plunder us.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 17 in full. The Damascus and Efrayim oracle is historically precise: both Damascus and the northern kingdom Efrayim were destroyed within decades by Assyria. Verse 7-8 — in that day man will look to his Maker and his eyes will look on the Holy One of Yisra'EL — is the covenant purpose embedded in the judgment: the destruction of the idols forces the covenant community back to their Maker.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 17 in full. Verse 10 — you have forgotten the Creator of your salvation and have not remembered the Rock of your refuge — is read as the covenant diagnosis of every form of apostasy in the prophetic corpus: forgetting YHWH is not intellectual error but covenant disloyalty.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 17. Verse 14 — at evening time, behold, terror — before morning, they are no more. The covenant reversal in a single night echoes the night the angel of YHWH struck 185,000 in the Assyrian camp in Melakhim Bet 19:35 — the same covenant event Yesha'yahu was prophesying.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 6 — gleanings will be left in it, as when an olive tree is beaten — two or three berries in the top of the highest bough. The covenant remnant theology appears even in the Damascus-Efrayim oracle: after the devastation, some remain. The gleaning image echoes Devarim 24:20 — when you beat your olive tree, do not go over it again — that which is left is for the sojourner. The covenant remnant is the gleaning that YHWH preserves when the harvest of judgment falls.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 18 The Oracle Concerning the Land of Whirring Wings — Beyond the Rivers of Kush

1 Ah, land of whirring wings that is beyond the rivers of Kush,

2 Which sends ambassadors by the sea, in vessels of papyrus on the waters. Go, you swift messengers, to a nation tall and smooth, to a people feared near and far, a nation mighty and conquering, whose land the rivers divide.

3 All you inhabitants of the world, you who dwell on earth, when a signal is raised on the mountains, look — when a trumpet is blown, hear.

4 For thus YHWH said to me: I will quietly look from my dwelling like clear heat in sunshine, like a cloud of dew in the heat of harvest.

5 For before the harvest, when the blossom is over, and the flower becomes a ripening grape, he cuts off the shoots with pruning hooks, and the spreading branches he lops off and clears away.

6 They shall all of them be left to the birds of prey of the mountains and to the beasts of the earth. And the birds of prey will summer on them, and all the beasts of the earth will winter on them.

7 At that time tribute will be brought to YHWH of hosts from a people tall and smooth, from a people feared near and far, a nation mighty and conquering, whose land the rivers divide, to Mount Tziyon, the place of the name of YHWH of hosts.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 18 in full. The oracle concerning Kush — the land beyond the rivers of Kush, likely referring to the Kushite dynasty of Egypt that included Pharaoh Tirhakah mentioned in Melakhim Bet 19:9 — describes YHWH watching the international political maneuvering from his dwelling and acting at the decisive moment.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 18 in full. Verse 7 — tribute will be brought to YHWH of hosts from a people tall and smooth — from a nation mighty and conquering — to Mount Tziyon. The Ethiopian and Ge'ez tradition reads this oracle with particular covenant attention as a declaration that the Kushite peoples — associated with the Afro-Asiatic corridor — will bring tribute to YHWH's covenant mountain.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 18. Verse 4 — I will quietly look from my dwelling like clear heat in sunshine. The covenant image of YHWH's patient observation before acting is the theological bridge between the noise of the nations — verse 3 — and the decisive covenant act of verse 5-6.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4 — I will quietly look from my dwelling. The covenant response to international political crisis is not panic but patient covenantal observation. YHWH watches from his dwelling — the same covenant posture as the throne vision of Chapter 6 — while the nations send ambassadors and raise signals and blow trumpets. Before the harvest YHWH acts — at the moment of his choosing, not at the moment of human urgency. The covenant principle of divine timing versus human timing.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 19 The Oracle Concerning Egypt — YHWH Riding on a Swift Cloud — Egypt and Assyria Shall Worship Together

1 An oracle concerning Egypt. Behold, YHWH is riding on a swift cloud and comes to Egypt. And the idols of Egypt will tremble at his presence, and the heart of the Egyptians will melt within them.

2 And I will stir up Egyptians against Egyptians, and they will fight, each against another and each against his neighbor, city against city, kingdom against kingdom.

3 And the spirit of the Egyptians within them will be emptied out, and I will confound their counsel. And they will inquire of the idols and the sorcerers, and the mediums and the necromancers.

4 And I will give over the Egyptians into the hand of a hard master, and a fierce king will rule over them, declares the Lord YHWH of hosts.

5 And the waters of the sea will be dried up, and the river will be parched and dry.

6 And the rivers will turn foul, and the branches of Egypt's Nile will diminish and dry up — reeds and rushes will rot away.

7 There will be bare places by the Nile, on the brink of the Nile, and all that is sown by the Nile will dry up, be driven away, and be no more.

8 The fishermen will mourn and lament, all who cast a hook in the Nile, and they will languish who spread nets on the water.

9 The workers in combed flax will be in despair, and the weavers of white cotton.

10 Those who are the pillars of the land will be crushed, and all who work for pay will be grieved.

11 The princes of Tzoan are utterly foolish. The wisest counselors of Pharaoh give stupid counsel. How can you say to Pharaoh, I am a son of the wise, a son of ancient kings?

12 Where then are your wise men? Let them tell you that they might know what YHWH of hosts has purposed against Egypt.

13 The princes of Tzoan have become fools, and the princes of Nof are deluded. Those who are the cornerstones of her tribes have made Egypt stagger.

14 YHWH has mingled within her a spirit of confusion, and they have made Egypt stagger in all its deeds, as a drunken man staggers in his vomit.

15 And there will be nothing for Egypt that head or tail, palm branch or reed, may do.

16 In that day the Egyptians will be like women, and tremble with fear before the hand that YHWH of hosts shakes over them.

17 And the land of Yehudah will become a terror to the Egyptians. Everyone to whom it is mentioned will fear because of the purpose that YHWH of hosts has purposed against them.

18 In that day there will be five cities in the land of Egypt that speak the language of Kena'an and swear allegiance to YHWH of hosts. One of these will be called the City of Destruction.

19 In that day there will be an altar to YHWH in the midst of the land of Egypt, and a pillar to YHWH at its border.

20 It will be a sign and a witness to YHWH of hosts in the land of Egypt. When they cry to YHWH because of oppressors, he will send them a savior and defender, and deliver them.

21 And YHWH will make himself known to the Egyptians, and the Egyptians will know YHWH in that day and worship with sacrifice and offering, and they will make vows to YHWH and perform them.

22 And YHWH will strike Egypt, striking and healing, and they will return to YHWH, and he will listen to their pleas for mercy and heal them.

23 In that day there will be a highway from Egypt to Assyria, and Assyria will come into Egypt, and Egypt into Assyria, and the Egyptians will worship with the Assyrians.

24 In that day Yisra'EL will be the third with Egypt and Assyria, a blessing in the midst of the earth,

25 Whom YHWH of hosts has blessed, saying, Blessed be Egypt my people, and Assyria the work of my hands, and Yisra'EL my inheritance.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 19 in full. The Egypt oracle contains the most universalist covenant declaration in the entire prophetic corpus — verses 23-25: Egypt, Assyria, and Yisra'EL worship together on a highway between the two empires, with YHWH calling Egypt my people and Assyria the work of my hands. The covenant titles given exclusively to Yisra'EL are extended to the two greatest empires of the ancient world.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 19 in full. Verse 19-21 — an altar to YHWH in the midst of Egypt — the Egyptians will know YHWH and worship with sacrifice and offering — is read within the Ethiopian tradition as the covenant declaration of Egypt's ultimate covenant destiny. The Ge'ez-speaking Ethiopian church sees itself as part of this covenant fulfillment.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 19. Verse 25 — blessed be Egypt my people and Assyria the work of my hands and Yisra'EL my inheritance — is the theological climax of the entire oracles against nations section. YHWH claims the two covenant enemies of Yisra'EL with the same covenant language he uses for Yisra'EL itself.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 23-25 — in that day there will be a highway from Egypt to Assyria — Yisra'EL will be the third, a blessing in the midst of the earth — blessed be Egypt my people, Assyria the work of my hands, Yisra'EL my inheritance. The covenant universalism of this passage has no parallel in the ancient world. The two nations that enslaved and exiled Yisra'EL are declared covenant co-participants with Yisra'EL in YHWH's blessing. Egypt — my people — the covenant title of Yisra'EL from Shemot. Assyria — the work of my hands — creator language. The exclusive covenant relationship of YHWH with Yisra'EL is not dissolved but expanded to include the nations.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 20 The Sign of the Naked Prophet — Three Years Walking Barefoot — Egypt and Kush Shall Be Led Away

1 In the year that the commander in chief, who was sent by Sargon the king of Assyria, came to Ashdod and fought against it and captured it —

2 At that time YHWH spoke by Yesha'yahu the son of Amoz, saying, Go, and loose the sackcloth from your waist and take off your sandals from your feet, and he did so, walking naked and barefoot.

3 Then YHWH said, As my servant Yesha'yahu has walked naked and barefoot for three years as a sign and a portent against Egypt and Kush,

4 So shall the king of Assyria lead away the Egyptian captives and the Kushite exiles, both the young and the old, naked and barefoot, with buttocks uncovered, the nakedness of Egypt.

5 Then they shall be dismayed and ashamed because of Kush their hope and of Egypt their boast.

6 And the inhabitants of this coastland will say in that day, Behold, this is what has happened to those in whom we hoped and to whom we fled for help to be delivered from the king of Assyria. And we, how shall we escape?

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 20 in full. The sign of Yesha'yahu walking naked and barefoot for three years is one of the most demanding covenant sign-acts in the prophetic corpus — exceeded in duration only by Yehezkel's covenant sign-acts. The historical precision of verse 1 — the year that the commander sent by Sargon king of Assyria came to Ashdod — is confirmed by Assyrian inscriptions naming Sargon II's campaign against Ashdod in 711 BCE.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 20 in full. The three-year covenant sign of the naked prophet is read as the covenant demand that the prophetic ministry require embodied cost — not merely spoken declaration. The prophet's body becomes the covenant message.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 20. The historical note of verse 1 — Sargon the king of Assyria — is one of the specific royal names confirmed by Assyrian archaeology. The Taylor Prism and the Khorsabad inscriptions confirm Sargon II's campaigns in the region.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2-3 — YHWH commands Yesha'yahu to walk naked and barefoot — and he did so — for three years, as a sign and portent against Egypt and Kush. The covenant sign-act is not metaphorical but physical. The prophet's dignity — his sackcloth garment of prophetic authority — is removed. He walks in the vulnerability of the captive for three years to embody the coming fate of Egypt and Kush. The covenant ministry of Yesha'yahu is not only a ministry of words but of covenant solidarity with those who will suffer the judgment he announces.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 21 The Oracle of the Wilderness of the Sea — Babylon Has Fallen — The Oracle of Dumah — The Oracle of Arabia

1 The oracle of the wilderness of the sea. As whirlwinds in the Negev sweep on, it comes from the wilderness, from a terrible land.

2 A stern vision is told to me — the traitor betrays, and the destroyer destroys. Go up, O Elam — lay siege, O Media. All the sighing she has caused I bring to an end.

3 Therefore my loins are filled with anguish — pangs have seized me, like the pangs of a woman in labor. I am bowed down so that I cannot hear — I am dismayed so that I cannot see.

4 My heart staggers — horror has appalled me. The twilight I longed for has been turned for me into trembling.

5 They prepare the table, they spread the rugs, they eat, they drink. Arise, O princes — oil the shield.

6 For thus the Lord said to me: Go, set a watchman — let him announce what he sees.

7 When he sees riders, horsemen in pairs, riders on donkeys, riders on camels, let him listen diligently, very diligently.

8 Then he who saw cried out: Upon a watchtower I stand, O YHWH, continually by day, and at my post I am stationed whole nights.

9 And behold, here come riders, horsemen in pairs. And he answered, Fallen, fallen is Babylon — and all the carved images of her gods he has shattered to the ground.

10 O my threshed and winnowed one, what I have heard from YHWH of hosts, the Creator of Yisra'EL, I announce to you.

11 The oracle concerning Dumah. One is calling to me from Seir, Watchman, what time of the night? Watchman, what time of the night?

12 The watchman says: Morning comes, and also the night. If you will inquire, inquire — come back again.

13 The oracle concerning Arabia. In the thickets in Arabia you will lodge, O caravans of Dedanites.

14 To the thirsty bring water — meet the fugitive with bread, O inhabitants of the land of Tema.

15 For they have fled from the swords, from the drawn sword, from the bent bow, and from the press of battle.

16 For thus the Lord said to me: Within a year, according to the years of a hired worker, all the glory of Kedar will come to an end.

17 And the remainder of the archers of the mighty men of the sons of Kedar will be few, for YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL, has spoken.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 21 in full. The oracle of the wilderness of the sea — the enigmatic title for Babylon — contains the watchman's proclamation of Babylon's fall in verse 9: fallen, fallen is Babylon, and all the carved images of her gods shattered to the ground. This double declaration is one of the most dramatic single verses in the prophetic corpus.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 21 in full. The covenant watchman theology — verse 6-9 — YHWH appoints a watchman to report what he sees — is the covenant description of the prophetic function. The prophet watches from the covenant watchtower and announces what YHWH shows him.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 21. Verse 9 — fallen, fallen is Babylon — is the covenant proclamation that will be repeated in the later prophetic tradition as the standard declaration of Babylon's covenant fate.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3-4 — the prophet's physical response to the vision of Babylon's fall is anguish and dismay, not triumph. My loins are filled with anguish — pangs have seized me like a woman in labor — the twilight I longed for has been turned into trembling. The covenant prophet who announces the fall of the oppressor does not celebrate it. The destruction of Babylon — however deserved — produces in Yesha'yahu the same grief response as the oracles against Moav. The covenant prophet absorbs the weight of judgment into his own body before he speaks it.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 22 The Oracle Concerning the Valley of Vision — The Day of Tumult — What Ails You Now — Shevna and Elyakim

1 The oracle concerning the valley of vision. What do you mean that you have gone up, all of you, to the housetops,

2 You who are full of shoutings, tumultuous city, exultant town? Your slain are not slain with the sword or dead in battle.

3 All your leaders have fled together — without the bow they were captured. All of you who were found were captured, though they had fled far away.

4 Therefore I said: Look away from me — let me weep bitter tears. Do not labor to comfort me concerning the destruction of the daughter of my people.

5 For the Lord YHWH of hosts has a day of tumult and trampling and confusion in the valley of vision, a battering down of walls and a shouting to the mountains.

6 And Elam bore the quiver with chariots and horsemen, and Kir uncovered the shield.

7 Your choicest valleys were full of chariots, and the horsemen took their stand at the gates.

8 He has taken away the covering of Yehudah. In that day you looked to the weapons of the House of the Forest,

9 And you saw that the breaches of the city of Dawid were many. You collected the waters of the lower pool.

10 And you counted the houses of Yerushalayim, and you broke down the houses to fortify the wall.

11 You made a reservoir between the two walls for the water of the old pool. But you did not look to him who did it, or see him who planned it long ago.

12 In that day YHWH of hosts called for weeping and mourning, for baldness and wearing sackcloth.

13 And behold, joy and gladness, killing oxen and slaughtering sheep, eating flesh and drinking wine. Let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die.

14 YHWH of hosts has revealed himself in my ears: Surely this iniquity will not be atoned for you until you die, says the Lord YHWH of hosts.

15 Thus says the Lord YHWH of hosts, Come, go to this steward, to Shevna, who is over the household, and say to him:

16 What have you to do here, and whom have you here, that you have cut out here a tomb for yourself, you who cut out a tomb on the height and carve a dwelling for yourself in the rock?

17 Behold, YHWH will hurl you away violently, O you strong man. He will seize firm hold on you

18 And whirl you around and around, and throw you like a ball into a wide land. There you shall die, and there shall be your glorious chariots, you shame of your master's house.

19 I will thrust you from your office, and you will be pulled down from your station.

20 In that day I will call my servant Elyakim the son of Khilkiyahu,

21 And I will clothe him with your robe, and will bind your sash on him, and will commit your authority to his hand. And he shall be a father to the inhabitants of Yerushalayim and to the house of Yehudah.

22 And I will place on his shoulder the key of the house of Dawid. He shall open, and none shall shut — and he shall shut, and none shall open.

23 And I will fasten him like a peg in a secure place, and he will become a throne of honor to his father's house.

24 And they will hang on him the whole honor of his father's house, the offspring and issue, every small vessel, from the cups to all the flagons.

25 In that day, declares YHWH of hosts, the peg that was fastened in a secure place will give way, and it will be cut down and fall, and the load that was on it will be cut off, for YHWH has spoken.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 22 in full. The oracle concerning the valley of vision — likely referring to Yerushalayim itself — addresses the covenant response to crisis: while YHWH calls for weeping and mourning, the people celebrate and feast. Verse 22 — the key of the house of Dawid placed on Elyakim's shoulder — is the covenant image of delegated royal authority that will appear in the later prophetic tradition.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 22 in full. Verse 13 — let us eat and drink for tomorrow we die — is read as the covenant diagnosis of covenant despair: when the covenant community loses its eschatological hope, it collapses into present indulgence. The absence of covenant hope produces hedonism.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 22. Verses 20-24 — the elevation of Elyakim as the covenant steward who carries the key of the house of Dawid — is the covenant appointment narrative that functions as a covenant parallel to the appointment of covenant leaders throughout the Nevi'im.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11 — you did not look to him who did it, or see him who planned it long ago. The covenant critique of Yerushalayim's defensive preparations is not that the preparations were wrong but that they were made without reference to YHWH. The city collected water, strengthened walls, and counted houses — all practical preparations — but did not look to the one who planned the crisis long ago. Covenant preparation without covenant orientation is the specific failure Yesha'yahu identifies.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 23 The Oracle Concerning Tzor — Wail O Ships of Tarshish — YHWH Has Purposed It — Tzor Shall Be Forgotten

1 The oracle concerning Tzor. Wail, O ships of Tarshish, for Tzor is laid waste, without house or harbor. From the land of Kittim it is revealed to them.

2 Be still, O inhabitants of the coast — O merchants of Tzidon, your messengers crossed over the sea

3 And were on many waters. Your revenue was the grain of Shikhor, the harvest of the Nile — you were the merchant of the nations.

4 Be ashamed, O Tzidon, for the sea has spoken, the stronghold of the sea, saying: I have neither labored nor given birth. I have neither reared young men nor brought up young women.

5 When the report comes to Egypt, they will be in anguish over the report about Tzor.

6 Cross over to Tarshish — wail, O inhabitants of the coast.

7 Is this your exultant city whose origin is from days of old, whose feet carried her to settle far away?

8 Who has purposed this against Tzor, the bestower of crowns, whose merchants were princes, whose traders were the honored of the earth?

9 YHWH of hosts has purposed it, to defile the pompous pride of all glory, to dishonor all the honored of the earth.

10 Cross your land like the Nile, O daughter of Tarshish. There is no restraint anymore.

11 He has stretched out his hand over the sea. He has shaken the kingdoms. YHWH has given command concerning Kena'an to destroy its strongholds.

12 And he said: You will no more exult, O oppressed virgin daughter of Tzidon. Arise, cross over to Kittim — even there you will have no rest.

13 Behold, the land of the Chaldeans — this is the people that was not — Assyria destined it for wild beasts. They erected their siege towers, they stripped her palaces bare, they made her a ruin.

14 Wail, O ships of Tarshish, for your stronghold is laid waste.

15 In that day Tzor will be forgotten for seventy years, like the days of one king. At the end of seventy years, it will happen to Tzor as in the song of the prostitute:

16 Take a harp — go about the city, O forgotten prostitute. Make sweet melody, sing many songs, that you may be remembered.

17 At the end of seventy years YHWH will visit Tzor, and she will return to her wages and will prostitute herself with all the kingdoms of the world on the face of the earth.

18 Her merchandise and her wages will be holy to YHWH. It will not be stored or hoarded, but her merchandise will supply abundant food and fine clothing for those who dwell before YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 23 in full. The Tzor oracle closes the oracles against nations section of Chapters 13-23. Tzor — the great Phoenician maritime trading city — represents the covenant principle that commercial glory is as subject to YHWH's judgment as military and political power. Verse 9 — YHWH of hosts has purposed it, to defile the pompous pride of all glory.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 23 in full. Verse 18 — her merchandise and her wages will be holy to YHWH — not stored or hoarded, but her merchandise will supply abundant food and fine clothing for those who dwell before YHWH — is read as the covenant transformation of commerce: the wealth of the nations ultimately serves the covenant community.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 23. Verse 15 — Tzor will be forgotten for seventy years, like the days of one king — is the covenant precision of the oracle: a specific time period of covenant judgment followed by restoration to commercial activity.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9 — YHWH of hosts has purposed it, to defile the pompous pride of all glory, to dishonor all the honored of the earth. The Tzor oracle makes explicit the covenant principle behind the entire oracles against nations section: YHWH's purpose in each oracle is not merely political realignment but the systematic dismantling of human pride and glory. Every great power — Babylon, Aram, Moav, Efrayim, Kush, Egypt, Edom, Arabia, Tzor — falls subject to this covenant principle. The covenant God will not share his glory with human imperial or commercial grandeur.

THE GREAT APOCALYPSE — CHAPTERS 24 THROUGH 27

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 24 The Great Apocalypse — The Earth Is Defiled — The Covenant Is Broken — YHWH Will Reign on Mount Tziyon

1 Behold, YHWH will empty the earth and make it desolate, and he will twist its surface and scatter its inhabitants.

2 And it shall be, as with the people, so with the priest — as with the slave, so with his master — as with the maid, so with her mistress — as with the buyer, so with the seller — as with the lender, so with the borrower — as with the creditor, so with the debtor.

3 The earth shall be utterly empty and utterly plundered — for YHWH has spoken this word.

4 The earth mourns and withers — the world languishes and withers — the highest people of the earth languish.

5 The earth lies defiled under its inhabitants — for they have transgressed the laws, violated the statutes, broken the everlasting covenant.

6 Therefore a curse devours the earth, and its inhabitants suffer for their guilt. Therefore the inhabitants of the earth are scorched, and few men are left.

7 The wine mourns, the vine languishes, all the merry-hearted sigh.

8 The mirth of the tambourines is stilled, the noise of the jubilant has ceased, the mirth of the lyre is stilled.

9 No more do they drink wine with singing — strong drink is bitter to those who drink it.

10 The wasted city is broken down — every house is shut up so that none can enter.

11 There is an outcry in the streets for lack of wine — all joy has grown dark — the gladness of the earth is banished.

12 Desolation is left in the city — the gate is battered into ruins.

13 For thus it shall be in the midst of the earth among the nations, as when an olive tree is beaten, as at the gleaning when the grape harvest is done.

14 They lift up their voices, they sing for joy — over the majesty of YHWH they shout from the west.

15 Therefore in the east give glory to YHWH — in the coastlands of the sea, give glory to the name of YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL.

16 From the ends of the earth we hear songs of praise, of glory to the Righteous One. But I say, I waste away, I waste away. Woe is me. For the treacherous deal treacherously, the treacherous deal very treacherously.

17 Terror and the pit and the snare are upon you, O inhabitant of the earth.

18 He who flees at the sound of the terror shall fall into the pit, and he who climbs out of the pit shall be caught in the snare. For the windows of heaven are opened, and the foundations of the earth tremble.

19 The earth is utterly broken, the earth is split apart, the earth is violently shaken.

20 The earth staggers like a drunken man — it sways like a hut. Its transgression lies heavy upon it, and it falls, and will not rise again.

21 On that day YHWH will punish the host of heaven, in heaven, and the kings of the earth, on the earth.

22 They will be gathered together as prisoners in a pit — they will be shut up in a prison, and after many days they will be punished.

23 Then the moon will be confounded and the sun ashamed, for YHWH of hosts reigns on Mount Tziyon and in Yerushalayim, and his glory will be before his elders.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 24 in full. Chapters 24-27 — the so-called Isaiah Apocalypse — form a distinct section within the book, with cosmic scope that exceeds any individual national oracle. Chapter 24 opens with a universal covenant judgment: the entire earth emptied, defiled, and broken because the everlasting covenant has been transgressed — verse 5.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 24 in full. Verse 5 — the earth lies defiled under its inhabitants — for they have transgressed the laws, violated the statutes, broken the everlasting covenant — is read as the foundational covenant diagnosis of universal human failure: the covenant given at creation has been broken not merely by Yisra'EL but by all the earth's inhabitants.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 24. Verse 23 — YHWH of hosts reigns on Mount Tziyon and in Yerushalayim, and his glory will be before his elders — is the covenant climax of the universal judgment: after the cosmic dismantling of every human power structure, YHWH reigns from Tziyon.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5 — the earth lies defiled under its inhabitants — for they have transgressed the laws, violated the statutes, broken the everlasting covenant. The covenant diagnosis is cosmic in scope: the defilement is not limited to Yisra'EL's covenant violations. The entire earth under all its inhabitants is covenant-defiled because the everlasting covenant — the covenant made with all humanity and with creation itself — has been broken. The covenant theology of Yesha'yahu's apocalypse is creation-wide: YHWH's covenant claim is not merely on Yisra'EL but on the entire created order.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 25 YHWH's Praise for His Covenant Faithfulness — He Will Swallow Up Death Forever — The Veil Over All Nations Removed

1 O YHWH, you are my Creator. I will exalt you — I will praise your name, for you have done wonderful things, plans formed of old, faithful and sure.

2 For you have made the city a heap, the fortified city a ruin — the palace of foreigners is a city no more — it will never be rebuilt.

3 Therefore strong peoples will glorify you — cities of ruthless nations will fear you.

4 For you have been a stronghold to the poor, a stronghold to the needy in his distress, a shelter from the storm and a shade from the heat — for the breath of the ruthless is like a storm against a wall,

5 Like heat in a dry place. You subdue the noise of the foreigners — as heat by the shade of a cloud, so the song of the ruthless is put down.

6 On this mountain YHWH of hosts will make for all peoples a feast of rich food, a feast of well-aged wine, of rich food full of marrow, of aged wine well refined.

7 And he will swallow up on this mountain the covering that is cast over all peoples, the veil that is spread over all nations.

8 He will swallow up death forever, and the Lord YHWH will wipe away tears from all faces, and the reproach of his people he will take away from all the earth, for YHWH has spoken.

9 It will be said on that day, Behold, this is our Creator. We have waited for him, that he might save us. This is YHWH — we have waited for him — let us be glad and rejoice in his salvation.

10 For the hand of YHWH will rest on this mountain, and Moav shall be trampled down in his place, as straw is trampled down in a dunghill.

11 And he will spread out his hands in the midst of it as a swimmer spreads his hands out to swim, but YHWH will lay low his pompous pride together with the skill of his hands.

12 And the high fortifications of his walls he will bring down, lay low, and cast to the ground, to the dust.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 25 in full. Verse 8 — he will swallow up death forever, and YHWH will wipe away tears from all faces — is one of the most theologically significant covenant declarations in the entire Hebrew canon. The swallowing up of death — as death swallowed in Chapter 5:14 — is the covenant reversal of the fundamental consequence of covenant rupture.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 25 in full. Verse 6 — on this mountain YHWH of hosts will make for all peoples a feast of rich food, a feast of well-aged wine — is read in the Ethiopian tradition as the covenant declaration of universal covenant hospitality: the covenant meal on YHWH's mountain is not restricted to Yisra'EL but prepared for all peoples.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 25. Verse 7-8 — the veil spread over all nations removed — death swallowed up forever — tears wiped from all faces — YHWH has spoken. The covenant declaration is sealed with the covenant formula: YHWH has spoken. The reversal of death is not human aspiration but divine declaration.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 8 — he will swallow up death forever. The covenant declaration uses the same verb — bala, to swallow — that appeared in Chapter 5:14 where Sheol enlarged its mouth and swallowed the covenant community. What Sheol did to the covenant people, YHWH does to death itself. The covenant reversal is precise and terminal: death does not merely cease to threaten — it is itself swallowed. Verse 9 — this is our Creator, we have waited for him. The covenant response to the abolition of death is not surprise but recognition: the community that waited in the covenant darkness of Chapter 8:17 now recognizes the one for whom they waited.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 26 The Strong City — Trust in YHWH Forever — Your Dead Shall Live — In the Shadow of His Wings

1 In that day this song will be sung in the land of Yehudah: We have a strong city — he sets up salvation as walls and bulwarks.

2 Open the gates, that the righteous nation that keeps faith may enter in.

3 You keep him in perfect peace whose mind is stayed on you, because he trusts in you.

4 Trust in YHWH forever, for YHWH Creator is an everlasting rock.

5 For he has humbled the inhabitants of the height, the lofty city. He lays it low, lays it low to the ground, casts it to the dust.

6 The foot tramples it, the feet of the poor, the steps of the needy.

7 The path of the righteous is level — you make level the way of the righteous.

8 In the path of your judgments, O YHWH, we wait for you — your name and remembrance are the desire of our soul.

9 My soul yearns for you in the night — my spirit within me earnestly seeks you. For when your judgments are in the earth, the inhabitants of the world learn righteousness.

10 If favor is shown to the wicked, he does not learn righteousness. In the land of uprightness he deals corruptly and does not see the majesty of YHWH.

11 O YHWH, your hand is lifted up, but they do not see it. Let them see your zeal for your people, and be ashamed. Let the fire for your adversaries consume them.

12 O YHWH, you will ordain peace for us, for you have indeed done for us all our works.

13 O YHWH our Creator, other lords besides you have ruled over us, but your name alone we bring to remembrance.

14 They are dead, they will not live — they are shades, they will not arise. To that end you have visited them with destruction and wiped out all remembrance of them.

15 But you have increased the nation, O YHWH — you have increased the nation. You are glorified — you have enlarged all the borders of the land.

16 O YHWH, in distress they sought you — they poured out a whispered prayer when your discipline was upon them.

17 Like a pregnant woman who writhes and cries out in her pangs when she is near to giving birth, so were we because of you, O YHWH.

18 We were pregnant, we writhed, but we have given birth to wind. We have accomplished no deliverance in the earth, and the inhabitants of the world have not fallen.

19 Your dead shall live — their bodies shall rise. You who dwell in the dust, awake and sing for joy. For your dew is a dew of light, and the earth will give birth to the dead.

20 Come, my people, enter your chambers, and shut your doors behind you. Hide yourselves for a little while until the fury has passed by.

21 For behold, YHWH is coming out from his place to punish the inhabitants of the earth for their iniquity, and the earth will disclose the blood shed on it, and will no more cover its slain.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 26 in full. Verse 19 — your dead shall live — their bodies shall rise — you who dwell in the dust, awake and sing for joy — is one of the clearest covenant declarations of bodily resurrection in the Hebrew canon, alongside Daniyel 12:2. 1QIsa-a preserves this verse clearly.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 26 in full. Verse 3 — you keep him in perfect peace whose mind is stayed on you, because he trusts in you — is read as the covenant formula of covenant peace: shalom shalom — perfect peace — is the covenant gift to the mind fixed on YHWH.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 26. Verse 4 — trust in YHWH forever, for YHWH Creator is an everlasting rock — is the covenant declaration of YHWH's eternal covenant reliability. The everlasting rock is the same covenant image as Devarim 32:4 — the Rock, his work is perfect.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 19 — your dead shall live — their bodies shall rise — you who dwell in the dust, awake and sing for joy. The covenant declaration of bodily resurrection is embedded within the covenant apocalypse of Chapters 24-27. The covenant logic: if death is swallowed up forever — Chapter 25:8 — then those already dead must rise. The covenant reversal of death extends backward into the past as well as forward into the future. Verse 20 — come, my people, enter your chambers. The covenant community is invited into covenant hiddenness while YHWH's fury passes — the same covenant pattern as the Passover in Shemot 12: blood on the doorposts, stay inside, let YHWH act.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 27 YHWH Will Slay Leviathan — The Pleasant Vineyard — The Scattered People Gathered From the Euphrates to Egypt

1 In that day YHWH with his hard and great and strong sword will punish Leviathan the fleeing serpent, Leviathan the twisting serpent, and he will slay the dragon that is in the sea.

2 In that day, a pleasant vineyard — sing of it.

3 I, YHWH, am its keeper — every moment I water it. Lest anyone punish it, I keep it night and day.

4 I have no wrath. If it sets briers and thorns against me in battle, I will march against them and burn them up together.

5 Or let them lay hold of my protection, let them make peace with me, let them make peace with me.

6 In days to come Yaakov shall take root — Yisra'EL shall blossom and put forth shoots and fill the whole world with fruit.

7 Has he struck them as he struck those who struck them? Or have they been killed as their killers were killed?

8 Measure by measure, by exile you contended with them. He removed them with his fierce breath in the day of the east wind.

9 Therefore by this the guilt of Yaakov will be atoned for, and this will be the full fruit of the removal of his sin: when he makes all the stones of the altars like chalkstones crushed to pieces, no Asherim or incense altars will remain standing.

10 For the fortified city is solitary, a habitation deserted and forsaken, like the wilderness. There the calf grazes — there it lies down and strips its branches.

11 When its boughs are dry, they are broken — women come and make a fire of them. For this is a people without discernment — therefore he who made them will not have compassion on them, he who formed them will show them no favor.

12 In that day YHWH will thresh out from the channel of the Euphrates to the Brook of Egypt, and you will be gleaned one by one, O people of Yisra'EL.

13 And in that day a great trumpet will be blown, and those who were lost in the land of Assyria and those who were driven out to the land of Egypt will come and worship YHWH on the holy mountain at Yerushalayim.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 27 in full. Chapter 27 closes the Isaiah Apocalypse with the slaying of Leviathan — the cosmic chaos serpent — and the gathering of the dispersed covenant people from Assyria and Egypt to YHWH's holy mountain. The covenant cycle is complete: judgment, cosmic upheaval, the death of death and chaos, and the return of the scattered covenant community.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 27 in full. Verse 2-3 — I, YHWH, am its keeper — every moment I water it — I keep it night and day — is read as the covenant reversal of the vineyard song of Chapter 5: the vineyard that YHWH abandoned to briers and thorns in Chapter 5 because it produced wild grapes is now kept by YHWH with constant care.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 27. Verse 13 — in that day a great trumpet will be blown — and those who were lost in Assyria and driven out to Egypt will come and worship YHWH on the holy mountain at Yerushalayim. The covenant gathering from the two great directions of exile — Assyria to the north, Egypt to the south — is the covenant completion of the dispersal announced throughout Chapters 1-23.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — YHWH will slay Leviathan the fleeing serpent, the twisting serpent, and the dragon in the sea. The covenant cosmic battle against the chaos dragon — whose imagery connects to the ancient Afro-Asiatic mythological tradition — is here claimed entirely by YHWH's covenant action. Leviathan is not a rival deity but a creature subject to YHWH's sword. The covenant declaration: the chaos that opposes YHWH's covenant order will be definitively slain. Verse 6 — Yaakov shall take root, Yisra'EL shall blossom and put forth shoots and fill the whole world with fruit. The covenant people scattered over the earth will become the source of the earth's fruit — the covenant people as the instrument of universal blessing promised to Avraham in Bereshit 12:3.

END OF PART TWO — CHAPTERS 13 THROUGH 27

Volume Seventeen — Yesha'yahu — Part Two
